title: Tapping for the Parent-Money Tangle
subtitle: A tapping practice for releasing the guilt, obligation, and role-reversal complexity of financially supporting ageing parents
excerpt: A five-minute tapping practice for the emotional complexity of supporting parents financially. The script works with the guilt, obligation, resentment, and love that tend to arrive tangled together in this situation — and moves toward giving from full rather than from duty.
sourceNotes: Original tapping script written for homemade.education. Uses Gary Craig's EFT framework (mid-1990s).

practice.whenToUse: Use when the financial care of ageing parents triggers guilt, resentment, obligation, or fear about your own financial security.

content.text: A five-minute tapping practice for the emotional tangle that arrives when parents need financial support. The feelings that show up in this situation tend not to be simple: guilt about having more, obligation that edges into resentment, love that doesn't resolve the logistics, and an undercurrent of fear about what it means for your own financial future. The script names the tangle and works toward giving from full rather than from duty.
New to tapping? Read
how EFT tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Even though I feel guilty that I have more than they do, and I don't know how to handle that, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though giving to them sometimes feels like obligation rather than love, and that makes me feel like a bad daughter, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I'm scared that supporting them will compromise what I've built, and I'm not allowed to say that out loud, I honour that fear and I am open to releasing it.
Tapping round
Eyebrow: the guilt about having more than the people who raised me.
Side of eye: the obligation that sits alongside the love, and I can't always tell them apart.
Under eye: the unspoken resentment I'm not supposed to feel.
Under nose: the fear that supporting them will undo the security I've spent years building.
Chin: I love them. The logistics are still complicated.
Collarbone: I am allowed to give and also allowed to have limits.
Under arm: I can care for them and still care for myself.
Top of head: the tangle is allowed to be complicated. My love is still real.
Reframe to positive
Eyebrow: I can give from what I have without guilt about having it.
Side of eye: I can love them and have limits, and those are not contradictions.
Under eye: my resentment is information, not a character flaw.
Under nose: my security and their care are not automatically opposed.
Chin: I can think clearly about the logistics while still loving them completely.
Collarbone: I give what I can. That is enough.
Under arm: I support them and I protect what I have built.
Top of head: I navigate this with love and with clear eyes.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. Uses Gary Craig's EFT tapping framework, in use since the mid-1990s.
